name: IB Physics
questions: Where does motion happen without any work being done?
Where does a force act perpendicular to motion, bending it into a curve?
Where does conservation of momentum shape what happens next?
Where do equations of motion predict what comes next from what's happening now?
Where does a rotating object behave like a translating one?
Where does the equilibrium of a system emerge from balanced opposites?
Where does a restoring force set the pace of a system?
Where do reference frames change the answer to 'what's happening'?
Where does air resistance change the story that idealized physics would tell?
Where does conservation of energy let you skip the kinematics?
Where does the behaviour of many particles give a system its macroscopic feel?
Where does a phase change absorb energy without a temperature change?
Where does the inverse-square law govern how something spreads out?
Where does the second law of thermodynamics set the direction of a process?
Where does the ideal gas model break down?
Where does thermal equilibrium determine the final state?
Where does a simplified model capture the essentials of a complex system?
Where does resistance turn ordered energy into disordered energy?
Where does superposition produce a pattern neither wave could make alone?
Where does a wave transfer energy without net movement of the medium?
Where does resonance amplify what a small driving force started?
Where does damping pull energy out of an oscillating system?
Where does a boundary change the wave that meets it?
Where does the Doppler effect reveal motion you couldn't otherwise see?
Where does wave behaviour make matter act unlike a particle?
Where does the wavelength of a wave decide what it can and cannot do?
Where does a standing wave pick out specific frequencies and reject others?
Where does a field let one object affect another across empty space?
Where does a potential tell you how much work it takes to move something?
Where do field lines reveal the geometry of a force?
Where does a charged particle's path bend because a field deflects it?
Where does induction turn motion into electricity?
Where does symmetry make a field easy to calculate?
Where does an equipotential surface cost no work to move along?
Where does the escape condition set a threshold on kinetic energy?
Where does a uniform field produce parabolic motion?
Where does an emission spectrum betray what something is made of?
Where does quantization force a system into discrete states?
Where does binding energy explain why a system holds together?
Where does mass itself become a source of energy?
Where does exponential decay describe how a population dwindles?
Where does a classical picture fail and a quantum one take over?
Where does wave-particle duality show light or matter acting as the other?
Where does equilibrium between gravity and pressure set a body's fate?
Where does a random process at the microscopic scale give a predictable rate?

options.text: Satellite in a circular orbit around Earth
options.why: Gravity is always perpendicular to velocity. No work done, orbit persists.

options.text: Puck sliding on frictionless ice at constant velocity
options.why: No force acts along the motion, so no work is done on the puck.

options.text: Box being pushed up a ramp
options.why: The push acts along the displacement. Work is very much being done.

options.text: Mass on a string swinging in a horizontal circle
options.why: Tension is radial, velocity is tangential. They're perpendicular, no work.

options.text: Charged particle circling in a uniform magnetic field
options.why: Magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity. Kinetic energy stays constant.

options.text: A falling apple accelerating toward the ground
options.why: Gravity acts along the motion. Work is done, kinetic energy grows.

options.text: Moon held in orbit by Earth's gravity
options.why: Gravity pulls inward perpendicular to the Moon's tangential velocity.

options.text: Electron in a cyclotron curving in a magnetic field
options.why: Magnetic force acts perpendicular to velocity, forcing a circular path.

options.text: Skydiver falling at terminal velocity
options.why: Forces are vertical and balanced. No curving, just straight-line motion.

options.text: Car rounding a banked curve at the design speed
options.why: The normal force's horizontal component supplies the perpendicular centripetal force.

options.text: Conical pendulum sweeping a horizontal circle
options.why: Tension's horizontal component pulls the bob toward the centre, perpendicular to motion.

options.text: Block on a spring oscillating back and forth
options.why: The spring force is along the motion, not perpendicular. It's SHM, not circular.

options.text: Rocket expelling exhaust gases
options.why: Momentum carried away by the exhaust equals momentum gained by the rocket.

options.text: Two billiard balls colliding on a table
options.why: With no external horizontal force, total momentum before equals after.

options.text: A ball being lifted slowly by a hand
options.why: An external force (the hand) acts on the system. Momentum isn't conserved.

options.text: Neutron-induced fission splitting a nucleus
options.why: The fragments' momenta must sum to the incoming neutron's momentum.

options.text: A gun recoiling as a bullet leaves the barrel
options.why: Equal and opposite momenta. The total starts at zero and stays there.

options.text: A pendulum swinging under gravity
options.why: Gravity acts externally on the bob. Momentum changes throughout the swing.

options.text: Projectile launched at a known angle and speed
options.why: Kinematic equations give position and velocity at any later time.

options.text: Car decelerating uniformly from a known initial speed
options.why: Constant acceleration means v = u + at predicts motion exactly.

options.text: A dice roll
options.why: Outcomes are random. No deterministic equation predicts the next value.

options.text: Ball dropped from a tower in vacuum
options.why: g is constant, so equations of motion give landing time and speed.

options.text: Object on a frictionless incline of known angle
options.why: Constant acceleration down the slope. Kinematics solves it completely.

options.text: Nuclear decay of a single unstable atom
options.why: The moment of decay is fundamentally random. No equation predicts it.

options.text: Angular momentum Iω behaving like linear momentum mv
options.why: Same conservation structure, one variable replaced by its rotational counterpart.

options.text: Rotational KE ½Iω² mirroring translational ½mv²
options.why: Moment of inertia plays the role of mass; angular speed plays the role of velocity.

options.text: Magnetic flux through a coil
options.why: A field-and-area quantity, not a rotational version of anything translational.

options.text: Torque τ = Iα as the rotational form of F = ma
options.why: Rotational inertia times angular acceleration, structurally identical.

options.text: Angular impulse τΔt producing ΔL, like FΔt producing Δp
options.why: Same impulse-momentum logic, rotational version.

options.text: Electric potential energy between two charges
options.why: A field interaction, not an analogue of any rotational equation.

options.text: Star balancing gravity against radiation pressure
options.why: Inward pull balanced by outward push keeps the star stable.

options.text: Object at terminal velocity in a fluid
options.why: Weight balances drag. Net force zero, velocity constant.

options.text: A ball rolling down a frictionless slope
options.why: Net force is nonzero. It's accelerating, not in equilibrium.

options.text: Book resting on a table
options.why: Normal force balances gravity. Static equilibrium.

options.text: Charged particle suspended between plates where electric force equals gravity
options.why: Two forces in opposition, perfectly cancelling.

options.text: An electron absorbing a photon and jumping to a higher level
options.why: A quantum transition, not an equilibrium of opposing forces.

options.text: Mass on a spring oscillating horizontally
options.why: Hooke's law gives F = -kx. The very definition of SHM.

options.text: Pendulum swinging at small angles
options.why: Gravity's restoring component is approximately proportional to displacement.

options.text: Steady current through a resistor
options.why: No oscillation, no restoring force. Just a constant flow.

options.text: Molecule vibrating in a diatomic bond
options.why: The bond acts like a spring. Restoring force sets the vibration frequency.

options.text: LC circuit exchanging energy between capacitor and inductor
options.why: A voltage-current analogue of mass-spring oscillation.

options.text: A satellite in geostationary orbit
options.why: Circular motion under gravity. Not an oscillation about equilibrium.

options.text: Time dilation between a fast-moving clock and a stationary one
options.why: Observers in different frames measure different elapsed times.

options.text: A ball appearing to curve for someone on a spinning merry-go-round
options.why: Non-inertial frame introduces apparent forces the ground observer doesn't see.

options.text: Charge of an electron measured in any lab
options.why: Invariant. The same value in every frame.

options.text: Simultaneity of two events depending on observer motion
options.why: Two events simultaneous in one frame aren't in another moving one.

options.text: Length contraction of a rod moving at relativistic speed
options.why: The rod's length depends on who's measuring it.

options.text: Mass of a proton at rest
options.why: A rest-frame invariant. Doesn't change with viewpoint.

options.text: Feather and coin falling in air reaching different speeds
options.why: Drag dominates the feather; in vacuum they'd fall identically.

options.text: Skydiver reaching terminal velocity rather than accelerating forever
options.why: Drag grows with speed until it cancels gravity.

options.text: Projectile launched on the Moon
options.why: Almost no atmosphere. Idealized physics applies with no drag correction.

options.text: Parachute slowing a descent
options.why: The whole point of the parachute is to maximize drag.

options.text: Low-orbit satellite gradually losing altitude
options.why: Residual atmosphere drags energy out, orbit decays.

options.text: Billiard ball collision on a polished table
options.why: Drag is negligible on the timescale of the collision. Idealized physics works.

options.text: Finding the landing speed of a dropped object from its height
options.why: ½mv² = mgh skips the need to track time at all.

options.text: Pendulum's max speed from its release height
options.why: All PE at the top becomes KE at the bottom. One equation, no kinematics.

options.text: Finding the time for a ball to fall a given distance
options.why: Energy conservation gives speed but not time. You need kinematics for time.

options.text: Roller coaster speed at the bottom of a hill
options.why: Just compare gravitational PE lost to KE gained. Fast and painless.

options.text: Maximum compression of a spring stopping a moving block
options.why: Set ½mv² equal to ½kx² and solve. No need to trace the block's path.

options.text: The distance a projectile travels horizontally before landing
options.why: Range needs the time of flight, which energy methods alone don't give.

options.text: Pressure of a gas from countless molecular collisions
options.why: Each collision is tiny; averaged over ~10²³, they produce steady pressure.

options.text: Temperature as the average kinetic energy of particles
options.why: A macroscopic quantity emerging from microscopic motion.

options.text: The charge on a single electron
options.why: A property of one particle, not an emergent ensemble behaviour.

options.text: Diffusion of a dye through water
options.why: Random walks of individual molecules sum to smooth spreading.

options.text: Electrical resistance from electrons scattering off the lattice
options.why: Microscopic scattering events averaged into a macroscopic resistance.

options.text: Planck's constant
options.why: A fundamental constant, not an emergent property of many particles.

options.text: Ice melting to water at 0 °C
options.why: Latent heat of fusion goes into breaking the lattice, not raising temperature.

options.text: Water boiling at 100 °C
options.why: Latent heat of vaporization separates molecules; temperature stays put.

options.text: Heating a block of iron from 20 °C to 50 °C
options.why: No phase change here. All heat goes into raising the temperature.

options.text: Dew forming on grass overnight
options.why: Vapour releases latent heat as it condenses, but its temperature doesn't drop during the change.

options.text: Dry ice subliming into gas
options.why: Solid CO₂ becomes gas at -78 °C. Heat absorbed, temperature constant through the change.

options.text: Warming soup on a stove
options.why: Liquid staying liquid. No phase change, so temperature rises steadily.

options.text: Gravitational force between two point masses
options.why: F ∝ 1/r². Newton's universal law.

options.text: Coulomb force between two point charges
options.why: F ∝ 1/r². Coulomb's law shares the geometry.

options.text: Force on a stretched spring
options.why: F ∝ x, a linear relationship. Nothing to do with inverse square.

options.text: Intensity of a point source of light
options.why: Energy spreads over a sphere of area 4πr², so intensity ∝ 1/r².

options.text: Sound intensity from a small loudspeaker in open space
options.why: Same geometry as light. Energy distributed over expanding spheres.

options.text: Magnetic force between two long parallel current-carrying wires
options.why: Force per unit length scales as 1/r, not 1/r². Different geometry.

options.text: Heat flowing from hot coffee to cool air, never the reverse
options.why: Entropy of the universe increases when heat flows down a gradient.

options.text: A gas rushing to fill an evacuated chamber when a valve opens
options.why: Free expansion raises entropy irreversibly.

options.text: A satellite in a circular orbit returning to the same point
options.why: A reversible cyclic process. Entropy doesn't pick a direction here.

options.text: An ice cube melting in a warm drink
options.why: Heat flows into the ice, raising total entropy. The process only runs one way.

options.text: A drop of ink spreading through water
options.why: Mixing is overwhelmingly more probable than unmixing.

options.text: A pendulum swinging in perfect vacuum with no friction
options.why: Idealized reversible motion. No preferred time direction.

options.text: Gas at very low temperature near liquefaction
options.why: Intermolecular forces matter now; ideal-gas assumption fails.

options.text: Gas under very high pressure where molecular volume matters
options.why: Finite molecular size breaks the point-particle assumption.

options.text: Dilute helium near room temperature and pressure
options.why: Precisely where the ideal gas law works best. No breakdown.

options.text: Water vapour on the verge of condensing
options.why: Attractive forces between polar molecules become significant.

options.text: A gas compressed to liquid-like densities
options.why: Molecules can no longer be treated as non-interacting points.

options.text: Air at sea level at 20 °C
options.why: Standard conditions. Ideal gas law predicts PV = nRT beautifully.

options.text: Hot metal block placed in cool water reaches a common temperature
options.why: Heat flows until both have the same temperature.

options.text: A cup of coffee on the counter cooling to room temperature
options.why: Equilibrium with surroundings sets the final value.

options.text: A battery driving current through a resistor
options.why: A steady-state process with continuous energy input. Not thermal equilibrium.

options.text: Planet's surface temperature set by incoming and outgoing radiation balance
options.why: Energy in equals energy out at equilibrium.

options.text: Two objects in contact eventually sharing one temperature
options.why: The zeroth law in action. Thermal equilibrium defines temperature.

options.text: A falling object accelerating under gravity
options.why: A dynamical process, not a thermal one.

options.text: Treating Earth as a point mass for orbital calculations
options.why: Distance dwarfs Earth's size. The simplification barely loses anything.

options.text: Ideal gas law for air at standard conditions
options.why: Ignoring intermolecular forces works brilliantly here.

options.text: Using quantum field theory to describe a falling apple
options.why: Wildly over-complicated. Simplification is the opposite direction.

options.text: Modelling a star as a black body
options.why: Real spectra have lines, but black-body captures total power and peak wavelength.

options.text: Massless, frictionless pulley in a textbook problem
options.why: Strips away inessentials so the principle shines through.

options.text: A detailed atmospheric model with every chemical species tracked
options.why: A complexification. The opposite of a simplification.

options.text: Current through a resistor heating a room heater
options.why: Electrical energy becomes random thermal motion. Ordered to disordered.

options.text: Friction heating a sliding brake pad
options.why: Kinetic energy of directed motion becomes heat in the pad.

options.text: A pendulum swinging in vacuum with no friction
options.why: No dissipation, no disordering. Energy stays ordered as KE-PE exchange.

options.text: Air resistance slowing a falling parachutist
options.why: Organized KE becomes heated air. Disordered thermal motion.

options.text: Viscous drag on a sphere falling through oil
options.why: Ordered fall energy dissipated as heat in the fluid.

options.text: A photon travelling through vacuum
options.why: No resistance, no dissipation. It just propagates.

options.text: Interference pattern from Young's double slits
options.why: Two coherent waves overlap producing bright and dark fringes.

options.text: Beats heard between two close musical tones
options.why: Sum and difference frequencies emerge from superposition.

options.text: Shadow cast by an opaque object
options.why: Geometric optics. No superposition of waves, just blocked light.

options.text: Standing wave on a guitar string
options.why: Two travelling waves going opposite ways superpose into nodes and antinodes.

options.text: Noise-cancelling headphones silencing engine hum
options.why: Inverted sound wave adds destructively to the incoming noise.

options.text: Refraction of light entering water
options.why: A single wave bending at a boundary. No superposition involved.

options.text: Sound wave carrying music across a room
options.why: Air molecules oscillate in place; energy travels through.

options.text: Ocean wave energy reaching a distant shore
options.why: Water circles in place while the waveform propagates.

options.text: A river flowing downhill
options.why: The medium (water) itself moves. This is bulk transport, not a wave.

options.text: Seismic S-wave travelling through Earth's crust
options.why: Rock oscillates transversely; energy moves outward from the quake.

options.text: Light from the Sun reaching Earth
options.why: No medium even required. The EM wave carries energy across the void.

options.text: A conveyor belt carrying packages
options.why: Material translation, not a wave. The belt itself goes somewhere.

options.text: Wine glass shattering at a singer's specific pitch
options.why: Driving frequency matches the glass's natural frequency. Amplitude grows.

options.text: Tacoma Narrows bridge oscillating wildly in moderate wind
options.why: Wind's shedding frequency hit a structural resonance.

options.text: Glass warming slowly in sunlight
options.why: Steady heating, no oscillating driver. Nothing resonant about it.

options.text: Tuning a radio to pick out one station among many
options.why: LC circuit resonates at one frequency; others pass unamplified.

options.text: A child on a swing going higher with timed pushes
options.why: Pushes at the natural frequency build amplitude each cycle.

options.text: A satellite in a stable orbit
options.why: Orbital dynamics, not resonance. No driving force at a natural frequency.

options.text: Car suspension using shock absorbers
options.why: Viscous fluid damps wheel oscillations so the ride doesn't bounce.

options.text: Pendulum in air slowly losing amplitude
options.why: Air drag dissipates KE each swing.

options.text: Standing wave on an ideal lossless string
options.why: No dissipation. Amplitude could persist indefinitely.

options.text: Ripples on a pond dying away
options.why: Viscosity and internal friction drain the wave energy.

options.text: Electrical resistance in an RLC circuit reducing oscillation amplitude
options.why: Joule heating dissipates electromagnetic oscillations.

options.text: An idealized frictionless mass-on-spring oscillating forever
options.why: No dissipation by assumption. Amplitude never decays.

options.text: Light refracting as it enters glass
options.why: Changed speed at the boundary bends the ray.

options.text: Wave partially reflected at a rope tied to a denser rope
options.why: Impedance mismatch sends some energy back.

options.text: Wave propagating through uniform air
options.why: No boundary, no change. It just travels.

options.text: Total internal reflection inside an optical fibre
options.why: Beyond the critical angle, the boundary turns the wave back entirely.

options.text: Sound echoing off a cliff wall
options.why: The boundary reflects the wave with little energy lost.

options.text: A steady laser beam in vacuum
options.why: Nothing for the wave to encounter. No boundary effect at all.

options.text: Redshifted light from a receding galaxy
options.why: Wavelength stretches. And astronomers read off the recession speed.

options.text: Ambulance siren pitch dropping as it passes
options.why: Frequency shift tells you the source was moving relative to you.

options.text: A stationary tuning fork at concert A
options.why: No relative motion. The Doppler effect has nothing to reveal.

options.text: Radar gun measuring car speed
options.why: Reflected microwave frequency shift gives the speed directly.

options.text: Doppler ultrasound imaging blood flow
options.why: Scattered ultrasound frequency shift maps blood velocity in vessels.

options.text: A hot object emitting thermal radiation
options.why: Black-body spectrum depends on temperature, not motion.

options.text: Electron diffraction through a crystal lattice
options.why: Electrons produce diffraction patterns. Pure particle pictures can't explain that.

options.text: De Broglie wavelength of a neutron used in scattering experiments
options.why: Matter has an associated wavelength that governs its scattering.

options.text: Billiard balls colliding on a table
options.why: Classical mechanics. No wave phenomena show up at this scale.

options.text: Interference pattern from a double-slit experiment with electrons
options.why: Each electron interferes with itself. Unambiguous wave behaviour.

options.text: Quantum tunnelling of alpha particles out of a nucleus
options.why: Particles leak through classically forbidden barriers because they're also waves.

options.text: A falling apple's trajectory
options.why: Classical, deterministic motion. No need for wave behaviour.

options.text: Using X-rays to image crystal atomic structure
options.why: X-ray wavelengths match atomic spacings. Diffraction reveals positions.

options.text: Ultrasound imaging of a fetus
options.why: Wavelength sets the minimum resolvable feature size.

options.text: A copper wire conducting DC current
options.why: No wave resolution limit involved. Just electron drift.

options.text: Radio waves passing through walls that block light
options.why: Long wavelengths diffract around obstacles where visible light doesn't.

options.text: Electron microscopes resolving features optical microscopes cannot
options.why: Electron de Broglie wavelengths are far shorter than visible light.

options.text: Friction slowing a sliding block
options.why: Mechanical dissipation. Nothing to do with wavelength.

options.text: Guitar string vibrating only at its harmonics
options.why: Boundary conditions allow only wavelengths that fit the string length.

options.text: Organ pipe resonating at particular notes
options.why: Air column's length selects a discrete set of allowed frequencies.

options.text: Ripples spreading from a stone dropped in a pond
options.why: A travelling wave, not a standing one. No frequency selection.

options.text: Microwave oven hot spots at fixed positions
options.why: Standing wave between the walls creates fixed nodes and antinodes.

options.text: Electron in a hydrogen atom restricted to discrete energy levels
options.why: Quantum standing-wave condition picks out the allowed orbitals.

options.text: Sunlight spreading through a prism into a continuous spectrum
options.why: Continuous range of frequencies. No selection, no standing wave.

options.text: Earth pulling the Moon across empty space
options.why: Gravitational field carries the influence without contact.

options.text: Static charge attracting nearby bits of paper
options.why: Electric field extends into the surrounding space.

options.text: Two gears meshed together transmitting torque
options.why: Direct physical contact. No field involved.

options.text: Compass needle aligning with Earth's magnetic field
options.why: The needle feels the field from thousands of kilometres away.

options.text: Sun's radiation warming a planet across tens of millions of km
options.why: EM field (waves) carries energy across the vacuum.

options.text: A hammer striking a nail
options.why: Contact force, right at the interface. Not a field-mediated interaction.

options.text: Voltage as work done per unit charge
options.why: Electric potential tells you the energy cost to move a charge.

options.text: Gravitational potential as work per unit mass at a point
options.why: Mirrors the electric case exactly. Work per unit.

options.text: Temperature of a gas
options.why: A measure of average kinetic energy, not work per unit of anything moved.

options.text: Height above ground as a proxy for gravitational potential
options.why: Near Earth's surface, gh = PE per unit mass.

options.text: Electric potential difference between capacitor plates
options.why: Work per unit charge to cross from one plate to the other.

options.text: Frequency of a photon
options.why: Relates to energy per photon, not work done on something to move it.

options.text: Iron filings aligning around a bar magnet
options.why: Filings trace out the field direction at each point visually.

options.text: Radial field lines around a single point charge
options.why: Outward arrows capture the inverse-square symmetric field.

options.text: A spectrum of light from a hot filament
options.why: Shows wavelength distribution, not field direction in space.

options.text: Parallel field lines between capacitor plates
options.why: Uniform field drawn as uniformly spaced parallel lines.

options.text: Curved field lines around a bent current-carrying wire
options.why: Loops follow the right-hand rule around the wire.

options.text: A wavefront diagram of a sound pulse
options.why: Shows crests in space, not the direction of a force.

options.text: Electron curving in a cathode-ray tube deflection coil
options.why: Magnetic force perpendicular to velocity bends the beam.

options.text: Cosmic ray spiralling along Earth's magnetic field lines
options.why: Lorentz force locks motion onto helical paths.

options.text: A cricket ball curving through air
options.why: The deflection is aerodynamic, not due to any electromagnetic field.

options.text: Positrons curving oppositely to electrons in a bubble chamber
options.why: Opposite charges bend opposite ways in the same magnetic field.

options.text: Electrons focused by an electrostatic lens in a microscope
options.why: Shaped electric fields redirect the beam.

options.text: A pendulum bob swinging under gravity
options.why: A neutral object in a gravitational field. No electromagnetic deflection.

options.text: Hydroelectric generator producing current from a spinning turbine
options.why: Rotating coil in a magnetic field induces an EMF.

options.text: Bicycle dynamo lighting a lamp
options.why: Wheel rotation drives a magnet past a coil, inducing current.

options.text: A solar panel powering a calculator
options.why: Photoelectric effect, not induction. No moving parts required.

options.text: Moving a magnet through a coil lighting an LED
options.why: Changing flux through the loop induces EMF directly.

options.text: Electromagnetic brakes slowing a train without contact
options.why: Conductor moving in a field induces currents that oppose motion.

options.text: A battery driving current through a circuit
options.why: Chemistry, not motion, is the energy source.

options.text: Field around a uniform sphere treated as from a point mass at the centre
options.why: Spherical symmetry collapses the calculation.

options.text: Uniform field between large parallel plates ignoring edges
options.why: Planar symmetry gives a constant E everywhere between.

options.text: Field around an irregularly-shaped lump of charge
options.why: No symmetry to exploit. The calculation is messy.

options.text: Magnetic field inside a long solenoid assumed uniform
options.why: Cylindrical symmetry plus length gives a clean result.

options.text: Field of an infinite line charge depending only on distance
options.why: Axial symmetry reduces the problem to one variable.

options.text: Field in a crumpled piece of conducting foil with random charge spots
options.why: Deliberately asymmetric. No simplification available.

options.text: Moving a charge along a conductor's surface
options.why: A conductor in equilibrium is all at one potential. No work needed.

options.text: Walking horizontally around a hillside at constant altitude
options.why: Gravitational equipotential. Height (and PE) doesn't change.

options.text: Lifting a book from the floor to a shelf
options.why: Height changes. You're crossing equipotentials, doing work.

options.text: Orbiting at fixed altitude around a spherical planet
options.why: A circle at constant r is a gravitational equipotential.

options.text: Moving a test charge around a field line perpendicular to itself
options.why: By definition, perpendicular to the force means no work.

options.text: Pushing a box along a rough horizontal floor
options.why: Friction does work even though gravity doesn't. Not work-free motion.

options.text: Spacecraft reaching Earth escape velocity of ~11.2 km/s
options.why: Below this, it falls back; above, it can leave Earth's well.

options.text: Gas molecules moving fast enough to leave a planet's atmosphere
options.why: Molecules with speeds above escape velocity don't come back.

options.text: A satellite in low-Earth orbit
options.why: Bound. It's orbiting, not escaping.

options.text: Black hole's event horizon marking the escape threshold for light
options.why: Even light needs escape velocity. And at the horizon, it exactly fails.

options.text: Alpha particle needing to tunnel out of the nuclear potential well
options.why: Insufficient classical energy. Only quantum tunnelling gets it out.

options.text: A pendulum oscillating within its well
options.why: Bound motion by definition. Escape is not in play.

options.text: Projectile launched at an angle across Earth's surface
options.why: Uniform g bends the path into a parabola.

options.text: Electron deflected as it crosses the gap between parallel plates
options.why: Uniform E produces parabolic motion, like gravity.

options.text: Satellite in a circular orbit far from Earth
options.why: Non-uniform (radial) field. Path is elliptical/circular, not parabolic.

options.text: Water arc from a garden hose in no wind
options.why: Uniform gravity plus initial velocity = parabolic stream.

options.text: Cannonball fired over a flat field
options.why: Near-surface uniform gravity produces the classic parabola.

options.text: Charged particle spiralling along a magnetic field line
options.why: Helical path from a magnetic field. Not a parabola at all.

options.text: Sodium street lamps emitting a characteristic yellow
options.why: Sodium's specific atomic transitions produce the 589 nm line.

options.text: Helium discovered in the Sun before being found on Earth
options.why: Solar spectrum lines identified an element nobody had isolated yet.

options.text: A hot glowing iron poker appearing red
options.why: Continuous black-body spectrum. Tells you temperature, not composition.

options.text: Hydrogen's Balmer series fingerprinting it in stellar atmospheres
options.why: A specific line pattern unique to atomic hydrogen.

options.text: Mercury vapour lamp's distinct blue-green line spectrum
options.why: Mercury atoms produce a signature set of wavelengths.

options.text: Light from an incandescent bulb
options.why: Thermal continuum. No discrete lines identifying any particular element.

options.text: Electron in a hydrogen atom sitting at discrete energy levels
options.why: Angular momentum quantization forbids in-between states.

options.text: Photons carrying energy only in multiples of hf
options.why: Light's energy comes in packets, not continuous amounts.

options.text: A ball rolling continuously down a slope
options.why: Classical motion. The ball takes every intermediate position.

options.text: Nuclear energy levels revealed by discrete gamma-ray lines
options.why: Nuclei have quantized states just like atoms do.

options.text: Vibrational modes of a diatomic molecule taking discrete values
options.why: Molecular oscillation energies come in steps of ℏω.

options.text: Rain filling a bucket smoothly over time
options.why: Continuous macroscopic process. No quantization visible.

options.text: Iron-56 sitting near the top of the binding energy per nucleon curve
options.why: Highest binding per nucleon means most stable against decay.

options.text: A deuteron held together by 2.2 MeV of binding energy
options.why: The system's mass is less than the parts' sum. Energy keeps them bound.

options.text: A baseball held in a catcher's glove
options.why: Mechanical grip, not a mass-energy binding.

options.text: Electron bound to a proton in the hydrogen atom by 13.6 eV
options.why: Ionization requires putting that energy back in.

options.text: Molecular bond energy in a water molecule
options.why: Breaking the bond takes the same energy that formed it.

options.text: Static friction preventing a book from sliding
options.why: A contact force threshold, not a binding energy in the nuclear/atomic sense.

options.text: Nuclear fission releasing energy as fragments' masses sum to less than parent's
options.why: Mass defect × c² appears as kinetic energy of products.

options.text: Sun converting hydrogen to helium, losing mass as radiation
options.why: 4 × ¹H mass exceeds ⁴He mass. Difference is E = mc² released.

options.text: Kinetic energy of a fast-moving car
options.why: Mechanical energy from work done, not from mass conversion.

options.text: Electron-positron annihilation producing two gamma photons
options.why: Total rest mass vanishes entirely into 2 × 511 keV of photons.

options.text: Pair production from a high-energy photon near a nucleus
options.why: Photon energy becomes the rest mass of a new electron-positron pair.

options.text: Gas pressure on a piston doing work
options.why: Thermodynamic work. No mass conversion involved.

options.text: Carbon-14 atoms in an old bone decaying by known half-life
options.why: N = N₀e^(-λt) describes the dwindling count over millennia.

options.text: Charge on a capacitor discharging through a resistor
options.why: Q(t) = Q₀e^(-t/RC). Same exponential shape.

options.text: Water level rising linearly in a bathtub with the tap on
options.why: Linear growth, not exponential decay.

options.text: Activity of a radioactive sample falling by half each T₁/₂
options.why: Classic exponential decay, with λ = ln2 / T₁/₂.

options.text: Light intensity in an absorbing medium decreasing with depth
options.why: I = I₀e^(-μx). Beer-Lambert law, same exponential form.

options.text: Population of bacteria doubling every 20 minutes
options.why: Exponential growth, not decay. Opposite sign in the exponent.

options.text: Photoelectric effect requiring a threshold frequency
options.why: Classical waves predict any frequency works given enough intensity. It doesn't.

options.text: Stability of atoms defying classical orbital collapse
options.why: Classical accelerating charges should radiate and spiral in; they don't.

options.text: A satellite's orbit computed with Newtonian gravity
options.why: Classical mechanics works to many decimal places. No quantum needed.

options.text: Black-body spectrum avoiding the ultraviolet catastrophe
options.why: Classical theory predicted infinite emission at short wavelengths. Quantization fixed it.

options.text: Tunnelling through barriers that classical particles couldn't cross
options.why: Classically forbidden, quantumly routine.

options.text: A billiard-ball collision at ordinary speeds
options.why: Classical momentum conservation handles it exactly.

options.text: Photons carrying momentum in Compton scattering
options.why: Light interacts particle-like with electrons. Wavelength shifts measurably.

options.text: Electrons producing a diffraction pattern through a crystal
options.why: Matter behaves as a wave to produce interference.

options.text: A rubber ball bouncing off a wall
options.why: Both classical and only mechanical. Duality doesn't feature.

options.text: Photoelectrons released one at a time by individual photons
options.why: Quantized particle-like energy delivery, not a continuous wave.

options.text: Neutrons used in diffraction experiments to probe crystal structure
options.why: Neutrons have a de Broglie wavelength suitable for atomic spacings.

options.text: A sound wave travelling through air
options.why: A pure wave phenomenon. No particle-like quantization observable here.

options.text: Main-sequence star balancing gravity with fusion-driven pressure
options.why: Steady state for billions of years while hydrogen burns.

options.text: Earth's atmosphere settling into a pressure gradient
options.why: Weight of air balanced by pressure below at every height.

options.text: A comet on a long elliptical orbit
options.why: Not an equilibrium. Constantly changing distance and speed.

options.text: White dwarf held up by electron degeneracy pressure against gravity
options.why: Quantum pressure, but the balance principle is the same.

options.text: Neutron star supported by neutron degeneracy pressure
options.why: Extreme case. Nuclear-scale density holds up the gravitational crush.

options.text: An electron in a hydrogen atom
options.why: Coulomb force, not gravity, is what matters; pressure isn't the counter.

options.text: Half-life of a radioactive isotope being highly reliable
options.why: Individual decays are random, but the rate over many atoms is fixed.

options.text: Gas pressure being steady despite chaotic molecular collisions
options.why: Huge numbers average out the randomness into a smooth value.

options.text: The outcome of a single coin flip
options.why: A single random event. No averaging, no predictable rate emerges.

options.text: Diffusion producing reproducible concentration profiles
options.why: Individual molecular random walks sum to a smooth, predictable spread.

options.text: Electric current flowing steadily through a wire
options.why: Individual electron paths are random; the average drift is reliable.

options.text: A ball's trajectory in projectile motion
options.why: Deterministic from the start. No randomness to average over.
